title: Is the 'No-Exams' Class Trend Actually Better for Learning?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are registering for classes and you notice something different. One section of the course you need has a traditional structure: two midterms and a cumulative final. Another section, taught by a different professor, has no exams at all. Instead, you will build a portfolio of projects throughout the semester. The no-exams option sounds appealing. No all-nighters in the library. No exam anxiety. No single test that determines a third of your grade. But is it actually better? Students who have taken these courses are divided. Some say portfolios reduce stress and deepen learning. Others say the workload is constant and relentless, with no breaks between high-stakes moments. Here is what the research says and how to decide which format works better for you.

What Portfolio-Based Grading Actually Looks Like

In a portfolio-based course, your grade is determined by a collection of work you produce over the semester. This might include essays, presentations, creative projects, lab reports, or reflective writing. Each piece is usually smaller than a traditional exam, but there are more of them, and they are spaced throughout the term. You might submit something every week or every two weeks. At the end of the semester, you compile your best work into a final portfolio that demonstrates your learning across the course.

The theory behind this approach is that learning is best demonstrated through application, not recall. Writing an essay that synthesizes course concepts shows deeper understanding than answering multiple-choice questions about those concepts. Building a model, creating a presentation, or designing an experiment requires you to use what you have learned, not just recognize it on a test. Proponents argue that portfolios assess the skills that actually matter beyond the classroom.

The Case for Exams

Exams have been the dominant form of assessment for centuries, and not entirely without reason. A well-designed exam tests whether you have mastered the core content of a course. It is a standardized measure. Every student faces the same questions under the same conditions. This standardization makes exams a fair way to compare performance across students and across semesters.

Exams also create a focused period of intense study. The pressure of an approaching exam motivates students to review and consolidate material in a way that weekly assignments may not. This cramming is not ideal for long-term retention, but the act of preparing for a comprehensive exam does force you to connect ideas across the entire semester, which has genuine learning value.

For some students, exams are simply more efficient. You study hard for a defined period, you take the test, and then you are done. The work is intense but bounded. There is a clear finish line. Portfolios, by contrast, can feel like a never-ending stream of deadlines with no break.

The Case for Portfolios

Portfolios assess a broader range of skills. Writing, analysis, creativity, and the ability to revise and improve your work are all part of the portfolio process. These are skills that translate directly to most professional careers. Few jobs require you to sit in a room and answer questions from memory. Many jobs require you to produce work over time, respond to feedback, and demonstrate growth.

Portfolios also reduce the impact of a single bad day. If you have a migraine on the morning of a final exam, your grade for the entire course suffers. If you submit a weak project in a portfolio-based course, it is one piece among many. You can recover. This reduces the high-stakes anxiety that makes exams so stressful for many students.

Research on the testing effect shows that retrieval practice, actively recalling information from memory, strengthens learning. Exams are one form of retrieval practice, but they are not the only form. Well-designed portfolio assignments can also require retrieval. A reflective essay that asks you to connect current course material to earlier concepts is a form of retrieval practice. The learning benefit comes from the retrieval, not from the exam format specifically.

The Drawbacks of Portfolios

The most common student complaint about portfolio-based courses is the workload. When every week brings a new deadline, there is no downtime. You cannot front-load your studying and then coast for a few weeks. The work is constant. For students juggling multiple courses, jobs, or family responsibilities, this steady pressure can be more stressful than periodic exams.

Portfolios also require strong time management skills. Without the structure of exam dates to organize your studying, you must pace yourself. Procrastination is punished more severely in a portfolio course. If you fall behind on weekly submissions, catching up is difficult because the next deadline is already approaching.

How to Decide Which Format Works for You

If you perform well under pressure and can focus intensely for short periods, traditional exam-based courses may suit you. If you prefer steady, consistent work and dislike having your entire grade rest on a few high-stakes moments, portfolio-based courses may be a better fit. Be honest about your work style. The format that feels easier is not always the one where you will learn more.

If you have a choice between formats for the same course, ask students who have taken both versions. Ask the professor how the portfolio is structured. A well-designed portfolio course includes clear rubrics, regular feedback, and opportunities to revise. A poorly designed one is just a collection of busywork with vague expectations. The quality of the design matters as much as the format.

The Bottom Line

The no-exams trend is not about making courses easier. It is about assessing different skills. Portfolios reward consistent effort, revision, and the ability to apply knowledge. Exams reward focused preparation and the ability to recall information under pressure. Both formats have strengths. Both have drawbacks. The right choice depends on how you learn best and what skills you want to develop. Whichever format you choose, the work of learning remains the same. No grading system can do it for you.
